NCDC1.pattern_interpretation:
PART 1: CURRENT STATE
CURRENT STATE  
Welcome. I’m William Maxwell, and I’m looking at your NCDC1 West11c baseline for January 2026.  
What you’re seeing in the big rectangle is the full energy field for the month—left to right is Jan 1 through Jan 31, and the height is your load magnitude.  
Dark blue at the top is what the transformer is being asked to deliver in kVA. Light blue is the real work you’re getting in kW. And that green layer at the bottom is reactive kVAR—energy you have to supply, but you don’t get to use.

Now, your zero line for performance is the target baseline.  
That dashed line is validated at **572.33 kVA**. So anything above that line is “meeting or beating target,” and anything below it is “under target.”

As I scan across the month, your supplied load is mostly living in the **roughly 450 to 550 kVA band**, with only brief touches above the target line. Weekends do dip, which is expected for cleaning and maintenance, but here’s the key pattern: even on many weekday slices, the field is still sitting under that 572 kVA target.  
Net result, your current operations score is **48.0% ElecOps** for the month—meaning you’re leaving a lot of usable electrical capacity on the table.

Up in the header, you can also see why the transformer is working harder than it needs to. Your **power factor is 82.6%**, and you’re carrying a meaningful reactive layer—plus elevated distortion. That waste doesn’t just stay “electrical.” It turns into heat and stress. You’re showing a **thermal burden of 302,616 BTU/hr**, and your **cooling and decay costs are $8.12 per hour**, which is about **44.2 kW** of continuous burden.  
And on the business side, the dashboard is already calling out the consequence: **total wasted value of $210,617 per year**.

So, you’re not looking at a “mystery problem.” You’re looking at a field that’s consistently under its target line, while also paying penalties in reactive demand and heat.  
Now let me show you the clean way we fix that—without asking you to change production behavior first.

PART 2: MPTS SOLUTION
MPTS SOLUTION  
Alright—scrolling into the solution, the first number I care about for sizing is your peak reactive demand.  
In your REACTIVE section, the average number is not what we size to. The number we size to is the smaller line: **355.8 kVAR MAX**. That’s your peak reactive requirement.

So here’s the straightforward sizing.  
A single **H490** unit is rated for **400 kVAR**, installed at **$200,000**. Your peak is **355.8 kVAR**, so **one H490** covers your maximum demand with headroom. Total installed cost: **$200,000**.

What that MPTS unit does, in plain terms, is it supplies the reactive power locally—right at the field—so your transformer and utility feed don’t have to push that “non-working” current anymore.  
Practically, that means your **power factor moves from 82.6% up to 99.5%**, and that green reactive layer you saw in the baseline collapses. When the reactive current disappears, the I-squared-R heating drops with it, and your cooling burden comes down.  

Now let’s look at what that means in results—visually and financially.

PART 3: EXPECTED RESULTS
EXPECTED RESULTS  
Now look at the after-state with MPTS in place.  
What you should notice first is how much cleaner your field becomes. The green waste layer that was riding under your load gets minimized, because the reactive demand is being handled locally. And your power factor line stabilizes up at the target—**99.5%**—which is what “harmonized” looks like in the real world.

Let’s anchor this in your actual baseline numbers.  
Before: **82.6% power factor**, **355.8 kVAR max reactive demand**, and **$8.12 per hour** in cooling-and-decay burden. You’re also carrying an energy-waste value that the dashboard annualizes at **$210,617 per year**, plus carbon impact—your CO2e cost input here is **$38.50 per metric ton**.

For savings and ROI, I’ll keep this clean and auditable using what your baseline screen already monetizes.  
If we treat that **$210,617 per year** as the recoverable waste value we’re targeting, that’s about **$24.04 per hour** in avoidable cost, on average.  
That rolls up to about **$17,550 per month** and **$210,617 per year**.

Against an installed MPTS cost of **$200,000**, your simple payback is about **11.4 months**—call it **roughly a one-year ROI**. And after payback, those savings don’t “end.” They keep compounding every month you operate, because you’re no longer buying and dissipating that reactive and thermal waste.

This is what electromagnetic stewardship looks like: cleaner power, lower heat, less waste, and a field that’s finally set up to hit—and stay above—your **572.33 kVA** performance line when production needs it.

NCDC2.pattern_interpretation:
PART 1: CURRENT STATE
CURRENT STATE  
Welcome. I’m William Maxwell, and I’m looking at NCDC2 South D with you.  
What you’re seeing on screen is your complete January energy field — the full rectangle is the whole month, left to right from Jan 1 to Jan 31. The height is load magnitude in kVA. So taller days mean heavier electrical demand, not “longer” days.

Up in the header, your delivered apparent power is averaging **652.9 kVA**, with **active power at 588.8 kW**, and your reactive layer is averaging **282.2 kVAR**. Your **power factor is 90.3%**, and harmonics are **5.5% iTHD**. Financially, the right side is putting a hard number on the waste: about **$162,511 per year** total wasted, and a thermal burden of **231,806 BTU per hour**. Cooling and decay is running about **$6.26 per hour**, which equals **33.9 kW** of cooling demand.

Now, the key visual anchor in the chart body is your dashed target line — the “zero line” for performance. Your validated baseline target for this transformer is **828.58 kVA**. So when your dark-blue supplied layer rises above that line, you’re meeting or beating the target. When it sits below, you’re under.

Let’s read the month like an operator. In the **left third of the chart — roughly Jan 1 through Jan 10 —** you’ve got several strong weekdays where supply peaks sit **above** that 828 kVA line, with a couple days pushing up near the **1,000 to 1,100 kVA** range. Then as you move toward the **middle of the month**, you can see the whole field “steps down” — the daily peaks flatten and spend a lot more time **under** the target line. And toward the **right half**, that lower plateau holds pretty consistently.

Weekends do what we expect — they drop to a lower, cleaning-and-maintenance profile. That part looks normal. What’s not normal is how many weekday production days are no longer reaching your baseline. Visually, I’m seeing **about 9 weekdays** that clearly peak **above** target, and **about 14 weekdays** that peak **below** target — with the under-target days commonly short by roughly **150 to 250 kVA** at the peak.

That pattern aligns with your operational score: your current **ElecOps is 53.4%** for the month. In plain terms, you’re running this transformer well below its validated electrical operating potential, and you’re paying for it in two places at once — first in avoidable waste layers, and second in heat you then have to remove. In a minute, when you scroll, I’ll show you the direct corrective move that tightens this field and removes the reactive drag that’s weighing down your baseline performance.

PART 2: MPTS SOLUTION
MPTS SOLUTION  
Alright — now let me show you how we fix what you’re seeing in that green layer.  
For MPTS sizing, we do not size off the average reactive number. We size off your peak. In the REACTIVE tile, the smaller line is the one that matters: your **kVAR MAX is 955.9 kVAR**.

That peak tells us the reactive “headroom” we have to cover so your transformer stops carrying waste current. And because **955.9 kVAR** is above 800, the correct answer here is a **custom Unity MPTS configuration** — it’s beyond the standard “two H490” ceiling. The standard products top out at **800 kVAR** with **2× H490**. So for this field, we design for at least **1,000 kVAR** of compensation capacity, so you’re covered at peak with margin.

Here’s what that means in plain language. MPTS injects the right reactive power, at the right timing, to cancel the lagging reactive draw created by motors, compressors, and inductive loads. When that reactive component is neutralized, your system stops wasting current. Your **power factor moves from 90.3% up to the 99.5% target**, the green layer collapses, and the transformer runs cooler and cleaner — with less electrical stress and less heat you have to pay to remove.

Now, as you scroll into the solution view, I’m going to compare what your field looks like before and after — and we’ll translate that into hourly, monthly, and annual dollars.

PART 3: EXPECTED RESULTS
EXPECTED RESULTS  
Now look at the “after” state — this is what a harmonized field looks like when MPTS is correctly sized to your real peak reactive demand. The first thing you notice is visual: that **green reactive waste band shrinks dramatically**, and the orange power factor line stabilizes right up near the top — because you’re no longer dragging the system with reactive current.

Let’s tie that to the numbers you just saw in the baseline. Baseline performance was **90.3% power factor** with **955.9 kVAR MAX**. With MPTS tuned to bring you to **99.5% PF**, you’re stripping out most of that reactive burden — and that directly reduces I²R losses, transformer heating, and the cooling load that follows.

Here’s the business side using your on-screen cost anchors. You’re currently showing **cooling and decay costs of $6.26 per hour**, and the dashboard is estimating **total wasted at $162,511 per year**. If we conservatively assume MPTS eliminates about **80%** of that waste component that’s attributable to reactive carry and related losses, your savings stack like this:  
- Estimated savings from waste: **0.80 × $162,511 ≈ $130,009 per year**  
- That’s about **$14.84 per hour** when spread across 8,760 hours.  
- Monthly equivalent: **$14.84 × 730 ≈ $10,833 per month**  
- Annual equivalent: **≈ $130,009 per year**

And you also get cooling relief. If even **half** of the **$6.26 per hour** cooling-and-decay load is driven by electrical waste heat that we can avoid, that’s an additional:  
- Cooling savings: **0.50 × $6.26 ≈ $3.13 per hour**  
So combined, you’re looking at roughly:  
- **Hourly savings:** **$14.84 + $3.13 ≈ $17.97 per hour**  
- **Monthly savings:** **$17.97 × 730 ≈ $13,118 per month**  
- **Annual savings:** **$17.97 × 8,760 ≈ $157,417 per year**

ROI depends on final custom configuration pricing, because your peak **955.9 kVAR** is above the standard catalog range. But to give you a practical planning number, if the installed solution lands around **$500,000** for a properly sized ~1,000 kVAR system, then your payback is:  
- **ROI period:** **$500,000 ÷ ($157,417 / 12) ≈ 38 months**

And here’s the key point: after payback, those savings keep compounding — and your baseline stability improves because you’re no longer operating the transformer as a reactive “heater.” That’s electromagnetic stewardship in practice: cleaner power, lower waste, lower heat, and a field that stays closer to your **828.58 kVA** performance target instead of sagging under it.

NCDC3.pattern_interpretation:
PART 1: CURRENT STATE
CURRENT STATE  
Welcome. I’m William Maxwell, and I’m walking you through NCDC3 North E.  
What you’re seeing on screen is your complete January energy field — the full rectangle is the whole month, from Jan 1 on the left to Jan 31 on the right, at one‑minute resolution. Up in the header are your electrical “vitals.” On the right are the business impacts — waste, heat, and cooling cost. And in the body, that stacked area chart is your real load behavior over time.

Now, your performance “zero line” for this transformer is a **target baseline of 548.03 kVA** — that’s your validated 95th percentile target. So as you watch the dark blue supplied layer rise and fall, everything **above** that line is over target, and everything **below** it is under target.

Visually, your field is pretty steady in shape — but it’s also telling us you’re leaving capacity on the table. Across the month, most weekday peaks are clustering **at or slightly under** that baseline instead of cleanly clearing it. I see only a handful of weekday moments that really jump above the target, and a larger share that top out below it. The weekends do what we’d expect — they dip down into lighter activity for cleaning and maintenance — that part looks normal.

Here’s the important operational takeaway. Your calculated **ElecOps is 37.9%** for the month. In plain language, that means you’re not converting the power you’re bringing in into productive work as efficiently as you should — and the chart shows why. In the header, you’re running **87.2% power factor**, with **223.6 kVAR of reactive on average**, and a **reactive peak we’ll size to in a moment**. That green layer at the bottom of the stack — that’s the unproductive reactive current driving losses and heat.

And the business impact is already quantified for you on the right. You’re carrying a **thermal burden of 211,153 BTU per hour**, and you’re paying **cooling and decay costs of $5.73 per hour**, which is tied to about **30.9 kW** of continuous overhead. On top of that, the dashboard is calling out **total wasted cost at $148,094 per year**, and an energy waste rate of **$7,661 per dataset**, which annualizes to about **$90,205 per year**.  

So the story of this baseline is simple. Your load shape is stable — but the field is not clean. You’re spending real money to move reactive power and turn it into heat. Now let me show you how we remove that green layer and push your field toward a near‑perfect power factor.

PART 2: MPTS SOLUTION
MPTS SOLUTION  
Alright — as you scroll into the solution view, the first number I care about is your **peak reactive demand**, because that determines MPTS sizing. In your header, in the Reactive section, the smaller line shows your **kVAR MAX: 417.8 kVAR**. That’s the peak reactive hit your transformer sees, and that’s what we have to cover.

So here’s the sizing. An **H490** is **400 kVAR** capacity, installed at **$200,000**. Your peak is **417.8 kVAR**, so one H490 alone is just short. We round up — we never undersize. The clean fit is:  
- **1 × H490 (400 kVAR)** plus **1 × H240 (200 kVAR)** = **600 kVAR total capacity**  
That gives you coverage for the peak with safe headroom.  
Total installed cost is **$300,000**.

What this does, in plain terms, is it supplies the reactive power locally — at the right phase angle — so your utility feed no longer has to deliver that waste current through your transformer. That’s how we lift your power factor from **87.2%** up to **99.5%**. And when that reactive current stops sloshing back and forth, two things happen immediately in your field: the green layer collapses, and your heat load drops — which directly reduces the cooling and decay penalty you’re paying every hour.

Now let’s go to what you should expect to see — visually and financially — after MPTS is in place.

PART 3: EXPECTED RESULTS
EXPECTED RESULTS  
Now look at the “after” field. This is the contrast I want you to focus on. The stacked shape stays — because your real production demand stays — but the field gets cleaner. That green reactive layer you saw in the baseline is largely removed, and the power factor line stabilizes up near **99.5%**. This is what a harmonized electrical field looks like: same work, less waste.

Let’s anchor that to your baseline numbers. In January, you had **417.8 kVAR MAX** reactive demand, and you were operating at **87.2% PF**. With MPTS sized at **600 kVAR** capacity, the reactive burden drops toward near‑zero at the transformer, and the system stops converting that waste into heat. Remember your baseline **thermal burden was 211,153 BTU per hour**, and your **cooling and decay cost was $5.73 per hour**. When we remove the reactive-driven losses, you should expect a meaningful portion of that hourly cooling overhead to fall away.

Now let’s do the business math with the numbers you already have on screen. Your dashboard is showing **total wasted cost of $148,094 per year**. If we treat that as the recoverable opportunity tied to power-quality waste, that’s about:  
- **$148,094 ÷ 8,760 ≈ $16.91 per hour** in avoidable cost  

So your savings frame is:  
- **Hourly savings:** **~$16.91/hr**  
- **Monthly savings:** **$16.91 × 730 ≈ $12,345 per month**  
- **Annual savings:** **~$148,094 per year**  

Against an installed MPTS cost of **$300,000**, your simple payback is:  
- **$300,000 ÷ $148,094 ≈ 2.03 years**, or about **24 months**.

And after payback, that savings doesn’t stop. It keeps compounding — month after month — because you’re no longer buying reactive waste, and you’re no longer paying to cool the heat that waste creates.  

That’s electromagnetic stewardship in practice. Cleaner field. Lower heat. Lower cost. And a transformer that spends its life doing real work for your operation, instead of carrying preventable electrical burden.

You’re carrying a **thermal burden of 211,153 BTU per hour**, and you’re paying **cooling and decay costs of $5.73 per hour**, which is tied to about **30.9 kW** of continuous overhead. On top of that, the dashboard is calling out **total wasted cost at $148,094 per year**, and an energy waste rate of **$7,661 per dataset**, which annualizes to about **$90,205 per year**.  

So the story of this baseline is simple. Your load shape is stable — but the field is not clean. You’re spending real money to move reactive power and turn it into heat. Now let me show you how we remove that green layer and push your field toward a near‑perfect power factor.
NCDC3.pattern_part2:
MPTS SOLUTION  
Alright — as you scroll into the solution view, the first number I care about is your **peak reactive demand**, because that determines MPTS sizing. In your header, in the Reactive section, the smaller line shows your **kVAR MAX: 417.8 kVAR**. That’s the peak reactive hit your transformer sees, and that’s what we have to cover.

So here’s the sizing. An **H490** is **400 kVAR** capacity, installed at **$200,000**. Your peak is **417.8 kVAR**, so one H490 alone is just short. We round up — we never undersize. The clean fit is:  
- **1 × H490 (400 kVAR)** plus **1 × H240 (200 kVAR)** = **600 kVAR total capacity**  
That gives you coverage for the peak with safe headroom.  
Total installed cost is **$300,000**.

What this does, in plain terms, is it supplies the reactive power locally — at the right phase angle — so your utility feed no longer has to deliver that waste current through your transformer. That’s how we lift your power factor from **87.2%** up to **99.5%**. And when that reactive current stops sloshing back and forth, two things happen immediately in your field: the green layer collapses, and your heat load drops — which directly reduces the cooling and decay penalty you’re paying every hour.

Now let’s go to what you should expect to see — visually and financially — after MPTS is in place.
NCDC3.pattern_part3:
EXPECTED RESULTS  
Now look at the “after” field. This is the contrast I want you to focus on. The stacked shape stays — because your real production demand stays — but the field gets cleaner. That green reactive layer you saw in the baseline is largely removed, and the power factor line stabilizes up near **99.5%**. This is what a harmonized electrical field looks like: same work, less waste.

Let’s anchor that to your baseline numbers. In January, you had **417.8 kVAR MAX** reactive demand, and you were operating at **87.2% PF**. With MPTS sized at **600 kVAR** capacity, the reactive burden drops toward near‑zero at the transformer, and the system stops converting that waste into heat. Remember your baseline **thermal burden was 211,153 BTU per hour**, and your **cooling and decay cost was $5.73 per hour**. When we remove the reactive-driven losses, you should expect a meaningful portion of that hourly cooling overhead to fall away.

Now let’s do the business math with the numbers you already have on screen. Your dashboard is showing **total wasted cost of $148,094 per year**. If we treat that as the recoverable opportunity tied to power-quality waste, that’s about:  
- **$148,094 ÷ 8,760 ≈ $16.91 per hour** in avoidable cost  

So your savings frame is:  
- **Hourly savings:** **~$16.91/hr**  
- **Monthly savings:** **$16.91 × 730 ≈ $12,345 per month**  
- **Annual savings:** **~$148,094 per year**  

Against an installed MPTS cost of **$300,000**, your simple payback is:  
- **$300,000 ÷ $148,094 ≈ 2.03 years**, or about **24 months**.

And after payback, that savings doesn’t stop. It keeps compounding — month after month — because you’re no longer buying reactive waste, and you’re no longer paying to cool the heat that waste creates.  

That’s electromagnetic stewardship in practice. Cleaner field. Lower heat. Lower cost. And a transformer that spends its life doing real work for your operation, instead of carrying preventable electrical burden.
